coredata.dc:title: PEI modified orange emissive carbon dots with excitation-independent fluorescence emission for cellular imaging and siRNA delivery
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Rui
Wei, Fangdi
Wu, Xueqing
Zhou, Ping
Chen, Qiutong
Cen, Yao
Xu, Guanhong
Cheng, Xia
Zhang, Aixia
Hu, Qin
coredata.dc:description: Although carbon dots (CDs) as a promising fluorescent nanomaterial have been studied for nearly twenty years, excitation-independent long wavelength emission CDs are rarely reported and are highly desirable for biomedical applications. Herein, we show that the excitation-independent, bright-orange-emissive, polyethylenimine-modified CDs (PEI-CDs) can be easily obtained by using 1, 2, 4-triaminobenzene and PEI as precursors through a simple hydrothermal method. PEI-CDs prepared under our experimental conditions are low-toxic and safe, and their cellular uptake can be observed via the red fluorescence of PEI-CDs appearing inside cells. Furthermore, PEI-CDs are able to bind spontaneously small interfering RNA (siRNA) to form the nano-complexes by electrostatic attraction. The PEI-CDs-siRNA complexes can effectively deliver the specific siRNA targeting hepatoma-derived growth factor (HDGF) into human glioblastoma cells, and therefore downregulate the expression of HDGF, which is considered as a potential target for glioblastoma treatment. Thus, the excitation-independent long wavelength emission PEI-CDs may constitute a new siRNA delivery carrier with imaging-trackable ability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: PEI-modified carbon dots
Long wavelength emission
Excitation-independent emission
Bioimaging
siRNA delivery
Glioblastoma treatment

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast footnotes grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vitro cellular uptake of PEI-CDs and PEI-CDs-siRNA complexes
The interaction of PEI-CDs with siRNA
Uptake of PEI-CDs and PEI-CDs-siRNA complexes by U251 cells
The inhibition effect of PEI-CDs-siHDGF complexes on HDGF expression

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis and purification of PEI-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Characterizations of PEI-CDs. (a) TEM image. (b) Size distribution histogram. (c) Full survey XPS spectra before and after etching. (d) XRD pattern. (e) FT-IR spectra. (f) Raman spectrum. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Optical properties of PEI-CDs. (a) UV–vis absorption, fluorescence emission and excitation spectra in ultrapure water. (b) Fluorescence emission spectra in ultrapure water at different excitation wavelengths (400–500 nm). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Gel retardation analysis of PEI-CDs-siRNA complexes. Ethidium bromide-stained agarose gel electrophoresis of PEI-CDs-siRNA complexes shows the siRNA binding ability of PEI-CDs. PEI-CDs-siRNA complexes at various weight ratio of PEI-CDs to siRNA (0.5 μg) ratios were electrophoresed at 100 V for 20 min. Following electrophoresis, the gel was stained with ethidium bromide.

In vitro cytotoxicity evaluation of PEI-CDs. U251 cells were incubated with PEI-CDs at the concentrations ranging from 0 to 500 μg/ml for 48 h at 37 °C and the cell viability was assessed by MTS assay. Bar graphs show the cell viability of U251 cells after treated with PEI-CDs (n = 3). The cell viability of untreated control cells was taken as 100%. Data are the mean ± SD.

Uptake of PEI-CDs and PEI-CDs-siRNA complexes by U251 cells. The U251 cells were treated with different concentrations of PEI-CDs or PEI-CDs-Cy3-siRNA complexes with a ratio of PEI-CDs to siRNA weight of 100:1 for 6 h at 37 °C. (a) Confocal images of 250 μg/ml PEI-CDs-treated cells collected in the range of 540–670 nm (PEI-CDs channel) under excitation of 448 nm, 458 nm, 488 nm, 514 nm. Scale bar: 50 μm. (b) Representative fluorescence images of PEI-CDs-treated cells. The cell nuclei were stained with the nuclear dye Hoechst 33258. Confocal images were collected in the range of 540–670 nm (PEI-CDs channel) under excitation of 458 nm and 420–500 nm (Hoechst channel) under excitation of 405 nm. Scale bar: 50 μm. (c) Bar graphs show the uptake of PEI-CDs by U251 cells (the ratio of PEI-CDs to Hoechst fluorescence intensities) (n = 3). Data are the mean ± SD. ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, between groups as indicated, ANOVA. (d) Representative fluorescence images of PEI-CDs-Cy3-siRNA complexes-treated cells. The cell nuclei were stained with the nuclear dye Hoechst 33258. Confocal images were collected in the range of 540–670 nm (PEI-CDs channel) under excitation of 458 nm and 420–500 nm (Hoechst channel) under excitation of 405 nm and 560–650 nm (Cy3 channel) under excitation of 545 nm. Scale bar: 50 μm.

The inhibition effect of PEI-CDs-siHDGF complexes on HDGF expression. U251 cells were transfected with PEI-CDs-siHDGF complexes or free siHDGF (each at a siHDGF concentration of 200 nM). Immunoblots show HDGF levels in the transfected U251 cells (top) and quantification of the data (bottom) (n = 3). Data are the mean ± SD. ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, compared with control; #p < 0.05, ##p < 0.01, between groups as indicated, ANOVA.
Comparison between the dialysis-precipitation, precipitation and dialysis method.

head.ce:title: PEI modified orange emissive carbon dots with excitation-independent fluorescence emission for cellular imaging and siRNA delivery

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 211166, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 211166, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Cardiovascular & Cerebrovascular Medicine, School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 211166, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Cardiovascular & Cerebrovascular Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Cardiovascular & Cerebrovascular Medicine, School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 211166, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu, 211166, PR China.

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Although carbon dots (CDs) as a promising fluorescent nanomaterial have been studied for nearly twenty years, excitation-independent long wavelength emission CDs are rarely reported and are highly desirable for biomedical applications. Herein, we show that the excitation-independent, bright-orange-emissive, polyethylenimine-modified CDs (PEI-CDs) can be easily obtained by using 1, 2, 4-triaminobenzene and PEI as precursors through a simple hydrothermal method. PEI-CDs prepared under our experimental conditions are low-toxic and safe, and their cellular uptake can be observed via the red fluorescence of PEI-CDs appearing inside cells. Furthermore, PEI-CDs are able to bind spontaneously small interfering RNA (siRNA) to form the nano-complexes by electrostatic attraction. The PEI-CDs-siRNA complexes can effectively deliver the specific siRNA targeting hepatoma-derived growth factor (HDGF) into human glioblastoma cells, and therefore downregulate the expression of HDGF, which is considered as a potential target for glioblastoma treatment. Thus, the excitation-independent long wavelength emission PEI-CDs may constitute a new siRNA delivery carrier with imaging-trackable ability.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have drawn tremendous attention and triggered substantial investigation since 2004 [1]. Compared with commercial organic dyes and traditional quantum dots, CDs show significant advantages, especially bright fluorescence and excellent biocompatibility, which make them superior candidates for the drug delivery and bioimaging applications [2–4].

To improve and regulate fluorescence properties of CDs, surface modification and heteroatom doping have been frequently employed [5–9]. N is the most popular doping atom in CDs due to its compatible atom radius with C, high electronegativity, and unique electron configuration. It has been proven that N modified electron density by injecting electrons into CDs, thereby changing the internal electronic environment [10]. Many N-doped CDs have been prepared by employing various N sources as surface modification agents [11–13]. Among them, polyethylenimine (PEI) modified CDs (PEI-CDs) were one of the most intensively studied N-doped CDs [14–20].

PEI, a cheap and commercially available polymer, is comprised of repeating amine groups and highly soluble in water. It has already been widely applied to gene delivery owing to its significant positive charge and proton-sponge effect [21]. The molecular weights (MW) of PEI range from 800 to 25,000 Da. High MW PEI is proper for gene delivery, but it exhibits high cytotoxicity. Low MW PEI is less toxic, yet it has low transfection efficiency [22,23]. Fortunately, when high or low MW PEI was used to modify CDs, the obtained PEI-CDs had both low cytotoxicity and high transfection efficiency [14–16]. Wang et al. [14] illustrated a novel CDs based nanocarrier by using low MW amphiphilic PEI (Alkyl-PEI2k) for surface passivation. Alkyl-PEI2k-CDs nanocarrier had a markedly low toxicity and good gene transfection effect in vitro and in vivo. Pierrat et al. [15] fabricated PEI-CDs by pyrolysis of citric acid and branched PEI25k under microwave radiation. PEI-CDs proved efficient for small interfering RNA (siRNA) delivery to cells and high specific gene knockdown was achieved without associated cytotoxicity, contrary to what was observed with branched PEI25k. Liu et al. [16] constructed a high-efficient dual functional nano gene vector based on PEI-modified C-dots by onestep microwave assisted pyrolysis of glycerol in the presence of branched PEI25k. PEI-CDs could mediate gene transfection in COS-7 and HepG2 cells with higher or comparable efficiency as well as lower cytotoxicity relative to pristine PEI25k.

However, to the best of our knowledge, all the reported PEI-CDs showed intense emission only at blue-to green-light regions. According to the literatures, blue or green CDs are unfavorable for biological applications because blue and green emissions cannot penetrate tissue deeply and may cause the auto-fluorescence of biosamples [24–26]. Fortunately, recent studies show that long wavelength (i.e. yellow-to red-light) emission CDs (LWE-CDs) can not only own a strong penetrability to tissues, but also effectively avoid autofluorescence interference of biosamples [24–26].

There are two kinds of LWE-CDs: excitation-dependent and excitation-independent emissions. The emission spectra of excitation-dependent emission LWE-CDs change greatly with the variation of the excitation wavelength, and their emission wavelengths redshift when their excitation wavelengths increase. So, the common way to obtain long wavelength emission for excitation-dependent emission LWE-CDs is to increase their excitation wavelengths. However, this would induce the emission spectrum broader and the photoluminescence (PL) intensity weaker at longer wavelength, which restricts their further application in the biomedical field [27,28]. On the contrary, the shape of the emission spectrum of excitation-independent emission LWE-CDs remains invariable with the change of the excitation wavelength, and the optimum PL intensity of LWE-CDs can be obtained when excited at the maximum excitation wavelength [29,30]. This is very good for biomedical applications. So far, the reported excitation-independent PEI-CDs only emit blue or green light, and their long wavelength emissions are very weak [15]. It is highly desirable to prepare excitation-independent PEI-CDs with much longer wavelength emission than blue or green light.

As we know, purification process greatly affects the fluorescent property and subsequent application of CDs. To obtain pure CDs, the most commonly used method is column chromatography or dialysis [31–38]. However, the separation process of column chromatography is complicated, and dialysis takes a very long time (generally≥24 h). The productive yields of both methods are also very low.

In the present study, we prepared excitation-independent, bright-orange-emissive PEI-CDs by a one-step hydrothermal method using 1, 2, 4-triaminobenzene (TAB) as the carbon source and PEI as the surface modifier. PEI-CDs were purified by a novel and easy method, and the productive yield was high. Besides, we evaluated the cytotoxicity and cellular uptake of PEI-CDs, analyzed the interaction of PEI-CDs with siRNA, and investigated the gene knockdown effect of PEI-CDs-siRNA complexes. The research results indicated that PEI-CDs could act as multifunctional biocompatible material for cell imaging and gene delivery.

TAB and PEI were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Petroleum ether and absolute ethanol were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). siHDGF duplexes (5′-AACCGGCAGAAGGAGUACAAA-3′) and scrambled siRNA (siScrambled) were purchased from GenePharma (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was obtained from a PURELAB Classic water purification (Pall, USA). Fluorescence measurements were conducted by an F-4600 fluorescence spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were performed using a UV2450 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). FT-IR spectra were acquired with a Tensor 27 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). Raman measurements were conducted with a Thermo Fisher DXR Raman Microscope equipped with a CCD detector in backscattered configuration using a 10 × objective (Madison, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was investigated by a Thermo K-Alpha (USA). The TEM was recorded on a JEOL JEM-2100 (HR) transmission electron microscopy (Tokyo, Japan). Zeta potential was measured on Zetasizer Nano ZS90 nanoparticle size and zeta potential analyzers (BIO-RAD, America).

PEI-CDs were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method. 100 mg of TAB and 210 mg of PEI were dissolved in 7 ml of ultrapure water and ultrasounded until completely dissolved. The solution was transferred into a 20 ml Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. The autoclaves were heated to 130 °C for 3 h, and then naturally cooled down to room temperature.

For the purification of PEI-CDs, the obtained mixture was first subjected to dialysis (MW = 1000) against ultrapure water for 6 h. The dialysate was subsequently concentrated to 2 ml by rotary evaporation. Then, ethanol was added to decrease the polarity. Finally, petroleum ether was employed to precipitate PEI-CDs. After washed with petroleum ether three times, the precipitates were dried under vacuum at 60 °C.

The PLQY of PEI-CDs was determined by a widely accepted relative method [17,29]. Rhodamine B (PLQY = 0.89 in ethanol)) was chosen as the reference. The PLQY of PEI-CDs (in water) was calculated according to the following equation:
ϕ x
=
ϕ s
⋅
A s
A x
⋅
F x
F s
⋅
(
n x
n s
)
2
Where ϕ is the PLQY, A was the optical density, F was the measured integrated emission intensity, and n is the refractive index (1.33 for water and 1.36 for ethanol). The subscript “s” refers to the reference and ‘‘x’’ for the sample. In order to minimize reabsorption effects, absorption was always kept below 0.10 at the excitation wavelength (495 nm).

Human glioblastoma U251 cells were obtained from the Cell Bank of Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China), and cultured in Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM; Gibco, USA) supplemented with 10% (v/v) heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS; Gibco, USA) and 1% (v/v) penicillin-streptomycin (Gibco, USA) in a humidified ambient atmosphere with 5% CO2 at 37 °C. U251 cells were passaged every 2–3 days with 0.25% (w/v) trypsin-EDTA (Gibco, USA).

A gel retardation assay was used to evaluate the binding of siRNA with PEI-CDs. PEI-CDs and siRNA were mixed at various weight ratios and incubated for 30 min at room temperature. The prepared PEI-CDs-siRNA complexes were loaded onto a 1% (w/v) agarose gel containing ethidium bromide and run in 1 × Tris-Borate-EDTA (TBE) buffer at 100 V for 20 min [39]. The gel was visualized using an ultraviolet transilluminator.

The U251 cells were plated at 5 × 103 cells per well in 96-well plates and allowed to attach overnight. The cells were treated with serum-free medium supplemented with different concentrations of PEI-CDs. After a 48 h incubation, the cells were washed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) for three times and the cell viability was assessed by 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-5-(3-carboxymethoxyphenyl)-2-(4-sulfophenyl)-2H-tetrazolium (MTS) assay according to the manufacturer’s protocol (Promega, USA) [40,41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cellular uptake of PEI-CDs and PEI-CDs-siRNA complexes
ce:section.ce:para: The U251 cells were seeded at 5 × 103 cells per well in 96-well plates and allowed to attach for 24 h. The medium was then replaced with 100 μl of serum-free medium containing varying concentrations of PEI-CDs. After 6 h of incubation, cells were washed with cold PBS, treated with 0.04% (w/v) trypan blue for quenching the extracellular fluorescence, and fixed in 4% (w/v) paraformaldehyde in PBS. The fluorescence of PEI-CDs (λ ex/λ em = 458/603 nm) uptaken into cells was detected using a SpectraMax M5 Multi-Mode Microplate Reader (Molecular Devices, USA). After that, cell nuclei were stained with 10 μg/ml Hoechst 33258, and Hoechst fluorescence (λ ex/λ em = 352/461 nm) in cell nuclei was also measured to stand for the cell numbers. The celluar uptake of PEI-CDs was expressed in the ratio of fluorescence intensities of PEI-CDs and Hoechst [42]. Images were obtained using a Leica STELLARIS 8 confocal microscope with a 63× oil-immersion objective (Leica, Germany).

The U251 cells were plated at 2 × 105 cells per well in 6-well plates and allowed to attach overnight. The cells were transfected with PEI-CDs-siHDGF complexes or naked siHDGF for 6 h, and then washed with fresh complete medium for an additional 48 h incubation before use. Then the cells were lysed in lysis buffer containing protease inhibitors and total proteins were extracted for Western blot analysis. Equal amounts of proteins were electrophoresed on a sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS) polyacrylamide gel and transferred onto a nitrocellulose membrane. The membrane was blocked with 5% (w/v) nonfat dry milk and incubated with a monoclonal anti-HDGF antibody. The bound primary antibodies were detected using a horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-conjugated secondary antibody and visualized using an enhanced chemiluminescence system (Pierce, USA) [43]. α-tublin was used as an internal control.
Data were presented as the mean ± SD. Comparisons among multiple groups were made using ANOVA followed by Tukey’s test. Differences were considered significant when p < 0.05.

PEI-CDs were synthesized through a hydrothermal method. TAB and PEI were selected as the precursors. In order to reduce the potential cytotoxicity of the resulting LWE-PEI-CDs, ultrapure water was used instead of other solvents such as formamide and dimethylformamide, which are usually used to prepare LWE-CDs [28,29].

The morphology of PEI-CDs was studied by TEM. It can be clearly seen that PEI-CDs are well dispersed without any aggregation (Fig. 2 a). High resolution TEM image taken from one particle dot showed an obvious lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (inset of Fig. 2a), which was attributed to the (100) facet of graphite and suggested PEI-CDs containing graphite-like structures [47]. Fig. 2b displayed that the size of PEI-CDs was between 5.15 nm and 7.85 nm with an average size of 6.50 nm.

XPS was used to investigate the chemical structure and composition of PEI-CDs. The full survey spectra showed three typical peaks at ∼285 (C1s), ∼398 (N1s), and ∼532 eV (O1s) before and after etching (Fig. 2c). The C, N and O contents of surface and bulk were 70.17%/77.46%, 23.23%/19.78% and 6.60%/2.76%, respectively (Table S2). The high-resolution XPS spectrum C1s demonstrated three peaks at 284.6 eV, 285.3 eV, and 287.3 eV, which were ascribed to C–C/CC, C–N, CN/CO, respectively (Fig. S2-1). The two peaks of N 1s spectrum at 398.7 and 399.7 eV were corresponding to pyridine-like N and pyrrole-like/amino N, respectively (Fig. S2-2). The O1s spectrum suggested the existence of CO (531.2 eV) and C–O–C (532.3 eV), respectively (Fig. S2-3) [29,44].
The XRD pattern of PEI-CDs (Fig. 2d) exhibited a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 21.7°, similar to the graphite lattice spacing, suggesting an amorphous nature [18].

FT-IR spectra of PEI and PEI-CDs are compared in Fig. 2e. The peaks of the N–H stretching vibration (3271 cm−1), C–H stretching vibration (2940, 2817 cm−1), C–H bending vibration (1457 cm−1) and C–N stretching vibration (1292 cm−1) can be seen in all the spectra. The peaks of the bending vibration of N–H are around 1587 cm−1 in the spectrum of PEI. However, it disappears in the spectrum of PEI-CDs. This may be due to the overlap with the stretching vibration of CC (1661 cm−1) arising from the carbonization process of PEI and TAB.

The Raman measurement also confirms the above analysis (Fig. 2f). The G-band (1586 cm−1) is attributed to the E 2g vibration mode that is present due to the sp2 bonded graphitic carbons. The D band (1365 cm−1) and D’ band (1620 cm−1) indicate a reduction in the symmetry of the graphitic lattice, caused by the incorporation of nitrogen atoms into the graphene lattice on substitutional sites [48–50]. Besides, the peaks at about 1510, 1434, 1221, 1140, and 941 cm−1 are ascribed to NN, C–O, C–C, pyrrole-ring breathing, and pyridine-ring breathing Raman signals, respectively [44].

The above results ascertain successful synthesis of PEI-CDs and verify the presence of –NH2 group on the surface of PEI-CDs. –NH2 not only shifts the emission of CDs to long wavelength but also preserves the high photoluminescence intensity inherent to CDs. The charges transfer from –NH2 to the CDs core, which narrows the energy gap of the π-π∗ transitions and further result in a small energy gap for the surface states [51]. Fig. 3 a showed that the PEI-CDs had a fluorescence emission wavelength of 603 nm, showing an orange color. PEI-CDs had a characteristic absorption bands located at 458 nm, which was mainly caused by the π-π∗ transition of aromatic CC bond. Fig. 3a also displayed that the fluorescence excitation band of PEI-CDs was overlapped with their absorption band. In addition, the fluorescence excitation correlation of PEI-CDs was examined. As the excitation wavelength was gradually changed from 400 nm to 500 nm, the position of maximum emission peak remained nearly invariable and the fluorescence intensity of PEI-CDs reached maximum when the excitation wavelength was at 450 nm (Fig. 3b). This excitation-independent photoluminescence in CDs is mostly due to the surface states/defects emission [52,53].

Owing to the fact that the stability of CDs is of great significance to their practical applications, the effects of pH value, ionic strength, longtime irradiation by UV light and storage time on the fluorescence intensity of PEI-CDs were investigated. Fig. S3-1 showed that the fluorescence intensity of PEI-CDs increased with the increase of pH from 3 to 12, which could be understood in terms of the change in surface charge owing to the protonation-deprotonation of –NH2 groups on the surface of PEI-CDs [17,47]. Fig. S3-2 exhibited the effect of ionic strength on the stability of PEI-CDs. It could be found that the fluorescence intensity of PEI-CDs kept almost invariable in a wide ionic strength range of 0–200 mM. The optical stability of PEI-CDs prepared under the optimized synthesis conditions was studied by irradiating PEI-CDs with ultraviolet light (345 nm) continuously and scanning the photoluminescence spectra of PEI-CDs at set interval (Fig. S3-3). It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity of PEI-CDs declines slowly when continuously irradiated with ultraviolet light for 5 h. After that, the fluorescence intensity begins to decrease obviously. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of PEI-CDs changes slightly when stored at 4 °C for 12 d (Fig. S3-4). Hence, it could be concluded that the as-prepared PEI-CDs had good chemical and photo-stability.

Fig. S3-5 exhibited that the zeta potential of PEI-CDs in ultrapure water was as high as +32.1 mV. Although pH value influenced the zeta potential of PEI-CDs obviously owing to the protonation and deprotonation of –NH2 on the surface of PEI-CDs, the zeta potential of PEI-CDs was up to +9.3 mV at pH 7.4 (Fig. S3-6). The positively charged surface of PEI-CDs has great potential to bind and condense the negatively charged siRNA by electrostatic interactions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The interaction of PEI-CDs with siRNA
ce:section.ce:para: As is known, the pH value of the normal environment in our body is around 7.4 [54], the nano-size particles are generally internalized into endosomes by cell endocytosis, and the pH value at endosomes can be as low as around 5.0 [55], therefore, pH 7.4 and 5.0 were selected in the experiment. The zeta potential of PEI-CDs was +9.3 mV at pH 7.4 and + 39.4 mV at pH 5.0 (Fig. S3-6). The zeta potentials of PEI-CDs-siRNA complexes with a ratio of PEI-CDs to siRNA weight of 100:1 at pH 7.4 and 5.0 buffer solution were +8.8 and + 24.4 mV, respectively (Fig. S4-1). The results suggested that PEI-CDs and PEI-CDs-siRNA complexes were able to buffer the low endosomal pH (pH down to around 5.0) and stimulate endosomal escape through the so-called proton-sponge effect [22,23], which facilitates release of siRNA cargo from acid endosomes to the cytosol (pH around 7.4) and is indispensable for nano-complexes entrapped in endosomes to acquire a satisfactory siRNA transfection.

Fig. S4-2 demonstrated that the fluorescence spectra of PEI-CDs kept unchanged after the addition of siRNA. The PEI-CDs-siRNA complexes also exhibited fine fluorescence stability against variation of ionic strength from 0 to 200 mM (Fig. S4-3). The size of PEI-CDs-siRNA complexes with a ratio of PEI-CDs to siRNA weight of 100:1 was between 33.8 nm and 66.2 nm with an average size of 49.8 nm (Fig. S4-4).

In order to further study the interaction of PEI-CDs with siRNA, gel retardation assay was performed. Free siRNA is negatively charged and therefore migrated toward the positive electrode, generating a bright band of migrated siRNA in the gel as shown in Fig. 4 . The migration position of siRNA complexed with PEI-CDs is the same as that of free siRNA when PEI-CDs are added to siRNA with a ratio of PEI-CDs to siRNA weight of 2.5:1 or 5: 1, suggesting that PEI-CDs had no effect on siRNA migration. When the ratio PEI-CDs to siRNA weight reaches 10:1, we can find the siRNA complexed with PEI-CDs migrates slightly slower than free siRNA, which is ascribed to a larger mass-to-charge ratio of PEI-CDs-siRNA complexes than free siRNA. When the ratio of PEI-CDs to siRNA is increased to 20:1 or 30:1, the migrated siRNA band disappears, indicating that the migration of siRNA complexed with PEI-CDs toward the positive electrode was completely retarded due to the further improved mass-to-charge ratio of PEI-CDs-siRNA complexes. Meanwhile, there seemed to be a movement of siRNA towards the negative electrode at and above the PEI-CDs to siRNA weight ratio of 20:1, probably because PEI-CDs complexed with siRNA had still a positive charge and was attracted by the negative electrode. These results suggested that a proper amount of PEI-CDs could bind siRNA to form stable complexes and hence might act as a siRNA carrier [56].

In this study, siRNA targeting hepatoma-derived growth factor (HDGF) (siHDGF) was chosen as the functional siRNA. HDGF has been considered as a novel potential therapeutic target for glioblastoma therapy [57]. Therefore, human glioblastoma U251 cells were used for the cytotoxicity, uptake and transfection experiments.

The cytotoxicity of PEI-CDs was assessed by MTS assay in U251 human malignant glioma cells. PEI-CDs did not alter cell viability significantly and mediate appreciable cytotoxicity until the final test concentration up to 500 mg/ml (Fig. 5 ). These results indicated that PEI-CDs had low cytotoxicity and acceptable safety profile under all the tested experimental conditions, and could be used as a siRNA carrier for further studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Uptake of PEI-CDs and PEI-CDs-siRNA complexes by U251 cells
ce:section.ce:para: As can be seen in Fig. 6 a, stronger red emission of PEI-CDs inside 250 μg/ml PEI-CDs-treated U251 cells is induced by the laser excitation at 488 nm and especial at 458 nm. On this basis, we noticed that under the laser excitation wavelength of 458 nm, bright red emission appeared in the cytoplasmic area and even nuclear region of 50 μg/ml and 250 μg/ml PEI-CDs-treated U251 cells, especially 500 μg/ml PEI-CDs-treated U251 cells, suggesting that PEI-CDs can pass through the biomembranes and enter into cells (Fig. 6b). Moreover, the cellular uptake of PEI-CDs displayed a concentration-dependent increasing trend in the concentration range 50–500 μg/ml, especially in the concentrations from 300 to 500 μg/ml (Fig. 6b and c). These results demonstrated that the as-synthesized PEI-CDs can label cells under the experimental conditions, and possess an imaging-trackable ability.

The Cy3 fluorescent dye was conjugated to siRNA (Cy3-siRNA) in order to determine the cellular delivery of siRNA loaded into PEI-CDs-siRNA complexes. U251 cells treated with PEI-CDs-Cy3-siRNA complexes with a ratio of PEI-CDs to siRNA weight of 100:1 exhibited Cy3 emission in their cytoplasm (Fig. 6d), which indicates that PEI-CDs are able to act as a siRNA delivery carrier and transport siRNA into U251 cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The inhibition effect of PEI-CDs-siHDGF complexes on HDGF expression
ce:section.ce:para: Glioblastoma is the most frequent and lethal human brain tumor with a poor median survival of less than 15 months, and current therapies provide no significant improvement in survival [58]. In recent years, rapidly accumulating data have demonstrated that HDGF is overexpressed in human glioblastoma, and its expression positively correlates with increasing degree of malignancy [59]. Silence of HDGF expression can inhibit glioblastoma growth and invasion [60,61]. Therefore, HDGF may serve as a promising therapeutic target for glioblastoma. Although there is currently no effective and specific inhibitors targeting HDGF, siRNA-mediated RNA interference (RNAi) provides an effective strategy for inhibiting this so-called undruggable target and developing a new therapy [62]. Nevertheless, free siRNA itself is difficult to penetrate through the cell membrane due to its high molecular weight and negative charge, and current siRNA delivery systems are still far from satisfactory [63]. In this study, we utilized the prepared excitation-independent long wavelength emission PEI-CDs as siRNA vehicle with imaging-trackable ability, hoping to achieve a safe and efficient delivery.

To further investigate the ability of PEI-CDs as a siRNA delivery carrier and the effect of PEI-CDs-siHDGF complexes on HDGF expression, human glioblastoma U251 cells which overexpress HDGF at the protein levels [64] were transfected with PEI-CDs-siHDGF complexes or free siHDGF, and the cell lysates were then subjected to Western blot analysis. As shown in Fig. 7 , naked siHDGF-treated cells have a similar HDGF/α-tublin ratio value to untreated control cells, suggesting that free siHDGF had no effect on HDGF expression. However, there was significantly reduced expression of HDGF in U251 cells after transfected with 250 μg/ml PEI-CDs-complexed and especially 500 μg/ml PEI-CDs-complexed siHDGF. The results indicated that free siHDGF was unable to enter into living cells without delivery vehicles. Unlike free siHDGF, PEI-CDs-siHDGF complexes formed in the proper ratio could effectively transport siHDGF into human glioblastoma cells to silence HDGF expression.

The PEI-CDs with unique characters were successfully prepared in aqueous solution by a one-step hydrothermal method. Compared with the reported method, our way is simpler, less time consuming and higher-yield. The results demonstrated that the PEI-CDs had high PLQY, bright-orange emission with independent excitation, positive surface charge, good chemical and photo-stability, which are significant and desirable for the application of PEI-CDs in biological studies. The research revealed that PEI-CDs could spontaneously bound siRNA to form a nano-complex by electrostatic attraction, and could act as an imaging-traceable siRNA delivery carrier. The PEI-CDs-siRNA complexes could effectively deliver siHDGF into human glioblastoma cells and hence downregulate HDGF expression. The research indicated that PEI-CDs could constitute a new siRNA delivery carrier with imaging-trackable ability. Certainly, the transfection efficiency of a delivery nano-complex is closely related to nano-complex size, surface charge, surface modification, aggregation, etc. PEI-CDs need to be further optimized, especially when used for systemic administration, to improve siRNA payloads and prevent aggregation and inhibit nonspecific delivery. Subsequently, we are going to develop PEI-CDs-based imaging-traceable in vivo siHDGF delivery systems for glioblastoma research and therapy.

Rui Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Fangdi Wei: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xueqing Wu: Investigation, Data curation, Visualization. Ping Zhou: Investigation, Data curation, Visualization. Qiutong Chen: Investigation, Data curation. Yao Cen: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Guanhong Xu: Formal analysis, Visualization. Xia Cheng: Resources. Aixia Zhang: Validation. Qin Hu: Conceptualization, Validation, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 61775099, 81973283, 21705080, 82071375), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20201353), Science and Technology Development Fund of Nanjing Medical University-Major Project (No.NMUD2018004), and R&D fund for Smart Health Technology Innovation of Nanjing Medical University and Jiangsu Salt Group (No. NMU-SY201801).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is/are the supplementary data to this article:

The optimization of the synthesis conditions of PEI-CDs, XPS characterization of PEI-CDs, the stability of PEI-CDs and the interaction of PEI-CDs with siRNA are presented in the supporting information section.
supplementary data supplementary data

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2021.02.069.
